coredata.dc:title: Flexible electrode fabricated from molybdate-loaded silk fabric for hydrogen production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jialong
Zhang, Xiaoning
Ju, Fucheng
Huang, Can
Donley, Carrie
Xiao, Peng
Xu, Lan
Zhang, Hongju
coredata.dc:description: The broad use of the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is impeded by challenges associated with the availability and high cost of precious metal-based catalysts. Carbon materials co-doped with transition metals and nitrogen have emerged as highly promising alternatives among non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts due to their advantageous features, such as cost-effectiveness, high activity, and excellent stability. This study focuses on incorporating low-cost sodium molybdate onto silk fabric, which serves as a sustainable precursor for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon material. This silk fabric is then carbonized to create a flexible electrode for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The resulting electrode exhibits the in situ growth of Mo2C and MoO3, and this mechanism facilitates a smoother conduction path for electrons. The resulting electrode not only possesses flexible characteristics but also exhibits a large specific surface area. Transmission electron microscopy analysis reveals the presence of carbon quantum dots, which actively contribute to the heightened catalytic hydrogen evolution activity of the prepared electrode. Experimental findings unequivocally showcase the outstanding electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution capability and operational stability of the fabricated flexible electrode in acidic and alkaline environments. This work substantiates the efficacy of using low-cost sodium molybdate-loaded silk fabric as a catalyst precursor, thereby providing robust support for the design and implementation of non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts for the HER. This approach advances the development and broader application of carbon-free fuels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized silk
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Flexible electrode
Molybdate
Carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the morphologies of silk fabrics before and after carbonization, as well as images illustrating the bending behavior of the samples post-carbonization. (a-c) CSM-1050–0; (d-f) CSM-1050–0.05; (g-i) CSM-1050–0.5; (j-l) CSM-850–0; (m-o) CSM-850–0.05; (p-r) CSM-850–0.5.

SEM images and EDS spectra. (a-d) CSM-1050–0; (e-h) CSM-1050–0.05; (i-l) CSM-1050–0.5; (m-p) CSM-850–0; (q-t) CSM-850–0.05; (u-x) CSM-850–0.5. Boxes indicate the zoomed-in regions. EDS spots are indicated by the white crosshairs.
XPS spectra of Mo 3d and N 1 s for all the samples.
Influence of temperature and sodium molybdate concentration on the content of Mo2C in molybdenum species and on the content of graphitic-N in nitrogen species.
(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (b) the corresponding pore size distributions for carbonized silk fabrics.

Impact of carbonization temperature and sodium molybdate dosage on the ID/IG values of the samples. An unpaired, two-tailed t-test was used for statistical analysis. Groups with different lowercase letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05), while groups with the same letter are not significantly different (P > 0.05).
HR-TEM images of samples. (a) CSM-850–0; (b) CSM-1050–0; (c-d) CSM-850–0.5; (e-f) CSM-1050–0.5.

HER electrochemical performance of carbonized silk fabric and commercial 40 wt% Pt/C characterized in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. (a) LSV comparison plots; (b) overpotential bar graph; (c) Tafel slopes; (d) Nyquist plots are depicted, with the corresponding equivalent circuit shown in the left inset. Additionally, the upper right inset provides an enlargement of the area denoted by the rectangle. Chronopotentiometry response (V–t curves) for (e) CSM-1050–0.5 and (f) commercial 40 wt% Pt/C over 10 h at a current density of 10 mA·cm−2; LSV comparison plot for (g) CSM-1050–0.5 and (h) commercial 40 wt% Pt/C before and after 1500 CV cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

HER electrochemical performance of carbonized silk fabric and commercial 40 wt% Pt/C characterized in 1 M KOH solution. (a) LSV comparison plots; (b) overpotential bar graph; (c) Tafel slopes; (d) Nyquist plots along with the insertion of the corresponding equivalent circuit. V–t curves for (e) CSM-1050–0.5 and (f) commercial 40 wt% Pt/C over 10 h at a current density of 10 mA·cm−2. LSV comparison plot for (g) CSM-1050–0.5 and (h) commercial 40 wt% Pt/C before and after 1500 CV cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.
Morphology of HEK-293 cells treated with the leaching liquor of each group on Days 1, 2, 3, and 5: (a-d) negative; (e-h) CSM-1050–0; (i-l) CSM-1050–0.5; (m-p) positive; scale bar: 20 µm.
Optical density readings of the cytotoxic study of different tested groups.
Tensile strength, elongation at break, and toughness modulus of each sample.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: An unpaired, two-tailed t-test was used for statistical analysis (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Values of Cdl and ECSAfor CSM-T-C in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.
Values of Cdl and ECSA for CSM-T-C in 1 M KOH solution.

head.ce:title: Flexible electrode fabricated from molybdate-loaded silk fabric for hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Resource Insects, College of Sericulture, Textile and Biomass Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Resource Insects
College of Sericulture, Textile and Biomass Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Resource Insects, College of Sericulture, Textile and Biomass Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, NC 27599, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, NC 27599, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft Condensed Matter Physics and Smart Materials, College of Physics, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft Condensed Matter Physics and Smart Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft Condensed Matter Physics and Smart Materials, College of Physics, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical & Testing Center, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The broad use of the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is impeded by challenges associated with the availability and high cost of precious metal-based catalysts. Carbon materials co-doped with transition metals and nitrogen have emerged as highly promising alternatives among non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts due to their advantageous features, such as cost-effectiveness, high activity, and excellent stability. This study focuses on incorporating low-cost sodium molybdate onto silk fabric, which serves as a sustainable precursor for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon material. This silk fabric is then carbonized to create a flexible electrode for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The resulting electrode exhibits the in situ growth of Mo2C and MoO3, and this mechanism facilitates a smoother conduction path for electrons. The resulting electrode not only possesses flexible characteristics but also exhibits a large specific surface area. Transmission electron microscopy analysis reveals the presence of carbon quantum dots, which actively contribute to the heightened catalytic hydrogen evolution activity of the prepared electrode. Experimental findings unequivocally showcase the outstanding electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution capability and operational stability of the fabricated flexible electrode in acidic and alkaline environments. This work substantiates the efficacy of using low-cost sodium molybdate-loaded silk fabric as a catalyst precursor, thereby providing robust support for the design and implementation of non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts for the HER. This approach advances the development and broader application of carbon-free fuels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels are increasingly incapable of meeting the growing energy needs of human society due to their nonrenewable nature and tendency to cause environmental pollution. Hydrogen energy, as a form of secondary energy, possesses advantages such as ecological compatibility and ample availability. In contrast to electrical energy, hydrogen energy exists in a material form, with high energy density and a straightforward storage methodology, making it an ideal choice for large-scale, long-term energy storage [1]. The hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), which utilizes electrochemical methods to electrolyze water and produce hydrogen gas, has attracted widespread attention as an important means of hydrogen production [1,2].

Commercial carbon-supported platinum (Pt/C) demonstrates exceptional catalytic activity in the HER. However, the high cost and limited availability of platinum metal considerably constrain its widespread application [3]. Developing non-precious metal electrocatalysts for HER that demonstrate high activity and long-term stability in both acidic and alkaline media remains a challenging area of research and development [4]. Research indicates that molybdenum carbide (Mo2C) and molybdenum trioxide (MoO3) exhibit outstanding HER performance [5–7]. In comparison to platinum, molybdenum, with its abundant presence on the Earth’s surface, emerges as a promising alternative in hydrogen production catalysts [8].

Silk protein, being a renewable resource, can yield nitrogen-rich carbonaceous materials through the process of carbonization, rendering it suitable for use as an electrode in the electrocatalytic HER [9,10]. However, most research uses regenerated silk fibroin as a precursor or employs activators during the carbonization process [9–13]. We believe that the extensive use of organic solvents during the preparation of regenerated silk fibroin, along with the use of activators during carbonization, may result in the loss of flexibility in carbonized silk; flexibility not only enhances the adaptability of the carbonized silk electrode to different shapes but also enables it to thrive in a broader range of reaction environments [14] and allows for the design of electrode materials with a high density of catalytic sites within a confined space. Furthermore, the preparation process for regenerated silk fibroin is often time-consuming and resource-intensive, posing challenges for large-scale production.

Developing a straightforward synthesis method for large-scale production of HER electrocatalysts poses another challenge [15]. Loading or coating functional materials onto the surface of carbonized silk fabric can enhance or improve specific properties [16–18]. However, with functional materials that are difficult to disperse, aggregation may occur, potentially affecting the performance of the carbonized silk fabric. We noticed that carbonized silk, acting as a carbon source, should exhibit reducing properties [19]. Therefore, in this work, sodium molybdate, which is easily dispersed in water, was loaded onto silk fabric and subsequently converted into Mo2C through carbonization. The prepared electrode, distinguished by its cost-effectiveness and facile synthesis, emerges as a promising candidate for an electrocatalyst in the HER, showcasing efficient water-splitting capabilities under acidic and alkaline conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the carbonized silk fabric
ce:section.ce:para: A sample of 19 momme silk satin fabric (Suzhou Sangong Technology Co., Ltd.) was cut into strips of 1.5 cm × 5 cm, and a 0.5 wt% sodium carbonate solution was prepared. The fabric was immersed in the boiling sodium carbonate (AR grade, Jinshan Chemical Test) solution at a ratio of 1:50 (silk fabric to the sodium carbonate solution, w/v) for 30 min to ensure comprehensive cleaning of its surface and complete removal of silk sericin. This cleaning and degumming procedure was repeated three times. Subsequently, the silk fabric underwent immersion in stirred deionized water for 30 min, repeated three times.

After organizing and smoothing, the silk strips underwent air-drying and complete immersion in deionized water, as well as 0.05 M and 0.5 M sodium molybdate solutions (AR grade, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.), in distinct groups. Next, the samples were agitated in a shaker with a rotation speed of 60 rpm and maintained at an incubation temperature of 25 °C for 6 h. Before carbonization, the samples were dried in a vacuum oven (DZF-6012, Shanghai Yiheng Technology Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The subsequent carbonization procedure followed a previously published protocol [20] and was conducted in a tube furnace. After carbonization, the samples were gently rinsed with deionized water three times. The resulting product is named CSM-T-C, where CSM represents molybdenum species-loaded carbonized silk fabric, T represents the carbonization temperature, and C represents the concentration of sodium molybdate solution for silk fabric immersion.

The tensile strength of the samples was assessed following the Chinese Standard GB/T 1798–2001. Before measurement, the samples underwent moisture equilibration in a constant temperature and humidity chamber (LHS-100HC, Shanghai Huitai Equipment Manufacturing Co., Ltd., China) set at 20 ± 2 °C and 65 % ± 5 %. Subsequently, the tensile strength of the carbonized silk fabric was determined using a universal mechanical testing machine (GTM-2100, Xie Qiang Instrument Manufacturing (Shanghai) Co., Ltd.). The measurement involved clamping the samples parallel to the direction of tension with a pulling speed of 150 mm·min−1. Three samples from each group were subjected to measurements. The tensile strength can be calculated using the following equation:
E =
L -
L o
L
× 100 %
where ε (%) represents the elongation at fracture, L0 represents the initial length of the carbonized silk fabric, and L is the length of the carbonized silk fabric at the point of fracture.

The fracture stress can be determined by dividing the fracture load (F) by the cross-sectional area (A):
σ =
F A
The toughness was determined by integrating the stress–strain curve between the initial and final points [20].

The microstructure of the carbonized silk fabric was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and the elemental composition of the sample surface was analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the chemical state of the elements present at the surface, using a monochromatic Al Kα source (hυ = 1486.6 eV) at a power of 150 W. All spectra were acquired using 30 eV pass energy and charge neutralizer, to prevent charging. The peak-fitting process for high-resolution XPS spectra was performed according to established procedures [21,22]. To elucidate the phase composition of the samples, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured using a Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) radiation source, with a scanning rate set at 0.02° s−1. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption analyses were conducted using a surface area and porosity analyzer to measure the surface area and pore structure of the carbonized silk fabric. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied to investigate the lattice plane distances and crystal structures.

The degree of graphitization in the carbonized silk fabrics was assessed using a Raman spectrometer. The test employed an excitation wavelength of 532 nm, covering a scanning range of 100 − 3500 cm−1, with a spectral resolution of 2 cm−1. Data analysis was conducted using Origin software (OriginLab Corporation, Northhampton, USA) according to a previously established methodology [23].

All electrochemical tests were conducted on an electrochemical workstation using a standard three-electrode system. The carbonized silk fabric served as the working electrode, and a graphite electrode served as the counter electrode. The reference electrode was a Ag/AgCl electrode under acidic test conditions and a mercury/mercuric oxide (Hg/HgO) under alkaline test conditions.

The voltage potential in this study was referenced to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). The potential initially measured on the Ag/AgCl scale (
V
Ag / A g C l
) was subsequently converted to the RHE scale (
V
RHE
) using the following equation [10]:
V
RHE
=
V
Ag / A g C l
+ 0.0592 × p H + 0.2224
while that from the Hg/HgO (
V
Hg / H g O
) to the RHE scale (
V
RHE
) was calculated using the following equation: [24]
V
RHE
=
V
Hg / H g O
+ 0.0592 × p H + 0.098
The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was evaluated using cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves, which were obtained by testing the electrode under a non-Faradaic region at different scan rates (20 − 100 mV/s). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted across a frequency range spanning 105 − 10−1 Hz, employing an amplitude of 5 mV, at a constant potential of − 200 mV.

During the experiment, a commercial 40 wt% Pt/C electrocatalyst (HiSpec 4000, JM) was used as a control. The preparation involved dispersing 21 mg of 40 wt% Pt/C powder in a mixture of 200 μL ethanol (AR grade, Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co. Ltd., China), 280 μL deionized water, and 20 μL 5 wt% Nafion® D520 dispersion to create an ink. The resulting mixture was then ultrasonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, 10 μL of the prepared ink was deposited onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (5 mm in diameter, 0.19635 cm2) using the drop-casting technique. This was done to equalize the amount of Pt atoms loaded per unit area on the glassy carbon electrode surface with the amount of Mo atoms loaded per unit area on the surface of the carbonized silk fabric (see Supplementary Materials, ‘Molar density calculation for Mo and Pt atoms on the electrode surface’, for details). This electrode was air-dried at room temperature before the measurements.

The cytotoxicity of the carbonized silk fabric was assessed using human embryonic kidney-293 cells (HEK-293, American Type Culture Collection) in vitro through a cell-counting kit-8 (CCK-8, Beijing Labgic Technology Co., Ltd, China) assay. For this purpose, 0.9 g each of CSM-1050–0 and CSM-1050–0.5 was weighed and immersed in 20 mL of Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, Gibco, USA) for 24 h at 37 °C to obtain the leaching liquor. The complete growth medium (90 % DMEM and 10 % fetal bovine serum (Zhejiang Tianhang Biotech, China)) served as the negative control, while a 0.064 mg/mL phenol (for molecular biology, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) solution served as the positive control. HEK-293 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 2 × 103 cells/well with 100 μL of complete growth medium and then cultured for 24 h at 37 °C, 5 % CO2, with humidity greater than 95 %. Subsequently, 10 μL of the complete growth medium, the leaching liquor from the samples, and the prepared phenol solution were introduced into the wells. Subsequently, the cells were co-cultured for 24, 48, 72, and 120 h, with medium changes performed every 24 h.

In comparing the silk fabric before and after carbonization (Fig. 1 ), a discernible reduction in size is evident for the samples following carbonization. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the generation of volatile substances, including HCN, NH3, CO2, CO, H2, N2, and H2O, originating from the nitrogen, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms within the silk during the carbonization process, resulting in material contraction [25,26].

The graphs reveal that the prepared carbonized silk fabric maintains notable flexibility and can undergo bending. This inherent flexibility enables the HER electrode to more effectively conform to the shape of the reaction vessel, optimizing spatial utilization and increasing the specific surface area. For instance, fashioning the electrode into a loop exemplifies this adaptation, thereby enhancing the overall efficiency of the HER process.

Notably, increasing the concentration of sodium molybdate used in the silk fabric soaking process to 1 M resulted in a loss of flexibility in the carbonized silk fabric, rendering it more susceptible to breakage (Supplementary Materials: Figure S1).

The samples underwent SEM morphological characterization and EDS elemental analysis. SEM images were obtained without the application of gold coating to any of the samples, indicating the excellent conductivity of the samples. As shown in Fig. 2 , the integrity of the silk-knitted fabric was preserved after carbonization. Compared to non-carbonized silk fabric (Supplementary Materials: Figure S2), the fibers of samples CSM-1050–0 and CSM-850–0 exhibited smoother surfaces, with fewer wrinkles and no visible nanoparticles. By contrast, the fibers of the other experimental groups had nanoparticles on their surfaces. Among the experimental groups, the nanoparticles from sample CSM-1050–0.5 exhibited an obvious three-dimensional feature. Image J was utilized to measure particle size from three areas of CSM-1050–0.5 and CSM-1050–0.05 (Supplementary Materials: Figure S3), yielding average sizes of 145.48 ± 41.88 nm and 143.33 ± 43.91 nm, respectively.

The average particle size on the surface of CSM-850–0.05 was 145.82 ± 33.43 nm. The average particle size did not significantly differ between groups CSM-850–0.05 and CSM-1050–0.05. Group CSM-850–0.5 had an average particle size of 201.48 ± 49.06 nm, the largest among the experimental groups. Notably, the nanoparticles of group CSM-850–0.5 featured a flake-like structure protruding from the interior of the particles. We attribute this morphological characteristic to the greater amount of MoO3 (Supplementary Materials: Table S2), which possesses a flake-like structure, within CSM-850–0.5 [27,28]. EDS analysis indicated the absence of a detectable Mo peak from the spectrum of CSM-T-0, while this peak increased in intensity with the increased dosage of molybdate loaded on the silk fabric.

Moreover, in the N 1 s XPS spectrum, the peak at 394.8 eV is attributed to Mo 3p [34,35], that at 398.7 eV corresponds to pyridinic-N, those at 399.1 − 400.5 eV are associated with pyrrolic-N [36,37], and those at 400.8 − 401.3 eV represent graphitic-N [37,38]. As depicted in Fig. 3, with an increase in carbonization temperature, the proportion of graphitic-N gradually increases, while the percentages of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N decrease. This observation implies that an increased carbonization temperature contributes to an enhanced degree of graphitization in the samples [39]. As graphite, renowned for its excellent conductivity, demonstrates remarkable electrocatalytic performance in the HER, a heightened degree of graphitization becomes advantageous for catalyzing the HER process [40]. However, the content of graphitic-N within groups CSM-1050-C and groups CSM-850-C was unsubstantial (Fig. 4; for the analysis of significant differences, refer to Supplementary Materials Table S4). This observation suggests that the quantity of sodium molybdate loaded onto the silk fabric negligibly impacts the graphitization of the prepared samples.
These results indicate that CSM-1050–0.5 is well suited for HER due to its highest active site count and graphitic-N content among all samples.

Fig. 5 a illustrates the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms obtained for the carbonized silk fabric samples. Notably, CSM-1050–0.5 manifests the most substantial N2 adsorption volume, closely followed by CSM-850–0.5. In addition, the shapes of N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms for CSM-1050–0.5 and CSM-850–0.5 samples display a combination of Type I and Type IV [41]. A distinct hysteresis loop is discernible within the relative pressure (P/P0) range spanning 0.45 − 1.0, suggesting the coexistence of mesopores and micropores [42]. At a P/P0 value of approximately 0.5, both curves exhibit a type H4 hysteresis pattern, affirming the presence of mesopores with uniform dimensions. As P/P0 approaches 1, the adsorption capacity for both CSM-1050–0.5 and CSM-850–0.5 increases significantly, indicating the presence of a minor fraction of macropores [43].

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms for samples CSM-1050–0.05 and CSM-1050–0 exhibit a subtle S-shaped curve characteristic of Type II isotherms, suggesting they are non-porous materials [44–46]. By contrast, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm for sample CSM-1050–0.05 shows a rapid, linear increase in adsorption, reaching near saturation within a narrow pressure range. This behavior is characteristic of Type I isotherms and indicates that it is either non-porous or has very low porosity [44,47]. Sample CSM-850–0 exhibits the lowest nitrogen adsorption volumes, indicating minimal porosity. The observed differences in the isotherms are likely due to variations in the initial loading of sodium molybdate on the silk fabric, and differences in the carbonization temperature.

The pore size distribution of all samples is graphically represented in Fig. 5b. Except for the CSM-850–0.5, the majority of samples exhibit pore sizes predominantly clustered within 5 nm. Notably, CSM-1050–0.5 displays the highest abundance of both micropores and mesopores within its structure. The specific surface area (SBET), calculated using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) theory, and the total pore volume (Vtot) obtained at P/P0 close to 0.988 for all samples are presented in Table S5. According to the data, both SBET and Vtot increased concomitantly when elevated carbonization temperatures and higher molybdate dosages were loaded to the silk fabric. The large surface and pore volume can provide an increased number of active sites for HER, thus enhancing the efficiency and activity of HER.

The mechanical properties of each sample set were evaluated, the results of which are presented in Fig. 6 and Table 1 . Under the same carbonization temperature, an increase in sodium molybdate concentration decreases the tensile strength, elongation at break, and toughness modulus of carbonized silk fabric, particularly for CSM-1050–0.5. This decrease is ascribed to the increased SBET and Vtot. Furthermore, the transformation of sodium molybdate on the surface of carbonized silk fibers into Mo2C or MoO3 during the carbonization process, leading to the formation of a heterogeneous structure, can also contribute to a further attenuation of the mechanical properties of the specimens.

When the same sodium molybdate dosage is used for silk fabric immersion, carbonized silk fabric prepared at a carbonization temperature of 1050 °C exhibits higher tensile strength, elongation at break, and toughness modulus compared to fabric prepared at a carbonization temperature of 850 °C.

As illustrated in Fig. 7 , Raman spectroscopy was used to investigate the influence of carbonization temperature and sodium molybdate dosage on the graphitization degree of the samples. The G peak in the spectra represents in-plane stretching vibrations of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, indicative of ordered graphite structures [48]. By contrast, the D peak corresponds to lattice defects in carbon atoms, representing sp3-hybridized carbon atoms and disordered graphite structures [48]. The degree of graphitization was assessed using the D/G peak intensity ratio (ID/IG), wherein a smaller ID/IG ratio indicates a higher degree of graphitization [40].

As shown in Fig. 8 , the ID/IG values of carbonized silk fabrics decreased with increasing carbonization temperature. Notably, samples from the CSM-1050-C group exhibited a smaller ID/IG value than those from the CSM-850-C group, indicating an increased graphitization. This observation corroborates with prior research findings that higher carbonization temperatures yield a higher degree of graphitization [39]. As graphitization strengthens carbon fiber [49], this may explain why samples carbonized at 1050 exhibited better mechanical properties than those carbonized at 850 with the same sodium molybdate dosage loaded. Furthermore, our results suggest that sodium molybdate dosage does not substantially impact the ID/IG value. These findings align with the results derived from the XPS analysis.

The crystal lattice structures of the samples were observed and analyzed through TEM. As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, no crystalline structures were found in samples CSM-850–0 and CSM-1050–0. Clear crystal lattice stripes with d-spacings of 0.228 nm, 0.38 nm, and 0.34 nm were observed in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of samples CSM-850–0.5 and CSM-1050–0.5, corresponding to the (101) plane of β-Mo2C [30,50], the (110) plane of MoO3 [51,52], and the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [53,54], respectively (Fig. 9c and d).

From the HR-TEM images of CSM-1050–0.5 (Fig. 9e and f), crystal planes with lattice spacings of 0.228 nm, 0.38 nm, 0.34 nm, and 0.208 nm are clearly observed, belonging to the (101) plane of β-Mo2C [30,50], the (110) plane of MoO3 [51,52], the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [53,54], and the (100) plane of carbon dots (CDs), respectively. It has been reported that β-Mo2C exhibits the best electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance among all four Mo2C phases [55]. In addition, CDs can significantly enhance the efficiency of electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution due to their high conductivity, favorable hydrophilicity, abundant active sites, and strong spatial confinement effect [56,57]. The aforementioned observations provide compelling evidence that CSM-1050–0.5 should demonstrate outstanding catalytic activity in the HER.

Previous investigations via TEM have demonstrated the hexagonal shape of β-Mo2C sheets [58]. Consequently, it is postulated that the hexagonal structure observed in the TEM images of CSM-1050–0.5 (Fig. 9e) encompasses a substantial quantity of β-Mo2C. Elemental composition analysis of the TEM-observed region was conducted using EDS. Notably, Mo was absent from CSM-T-0, whereas its presence was discerned in CSM-T-0.5 (Supplementary Materials: Figure S3).

Fig. 10 a displays the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) profiles of the commercial 40 wt% Pt/C electrocatalyst and carbonized silk fabrics from various groups in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. The overpotential at a current density of 10 mA·cm−2 (η10) was utilized to assess the electrocatalytic performance of each sample. The results reveal that CSM-1050–0.5 demonstrates the most efficient HER performance among all samples. Notably, increasing the sodium molybdate solution concentration and the carbonization temperature can enhance the hydrogen evolution performance. The superior catalytic performance of CSM-1050–0.5 can be ascribed to its larger specific surface area and higher Mo2C content, and the presence of CDs [6,33].

The HER kinetics of each sample was evaluated using the Tafel slope. A lower Tafel slope suggests better HER kinetic performance. Among all the carbonized silk fabrics prepared, CSM-1050–0.5 exhibited the smallest Tafel slope (Fig. 10c), indicating the fastest reaction kinetics, consistent with the results from the LSV curves. The Tafel slope values follow the order: 64.68 mV·dec−1 (Pt/C), 143.93 mV·dec−1 (CSM-1050–0.5), 160.34 mV·dec−1 (CSM-1050–0.05), 243.36 mV·dec−1 (CSM-850–0.5), 266.19 mV·dec−1 (CSM-850–0.05), 357.70 mV·dec−1 (CSM-1050–0), and 414.18 mV·dec−1 (CSM-850–0). It is evident that increasing the sodium molybdate loading enhances the reaction kinetics. Moreover, elevating the carbonization temperature also contributes to the accelerated kinetics of the HER. According to the Tafel slopes, the HER mechanism for carbonized silk fabric may involve the Volmer–Heyrovský process, with a rate-determining Volmer step [10,59]. The reaction can be described as follows [60]:
∗ +
H
+
+
e
-
→ H ∗
( V o l m e r
s t e p )
∗ + H ∗ +
e
-
→
H 2
( H e y r o v s k
y ́
s t e p )
where * represents the active sites of the surface. The Volmer step involves breaking the O–H bonds in water molecules, causing atomic hydrogen to adsorb onto the surface of carbonized silk fabric. Next, the Heyrovsky step occurs, where the adsorbed hydrogen combines with an electron and a water molecule. This process forms H2 bubbles, which then detach from the surface of carbonized silk fabric.

Through EIS testing, we acquired the charge transfer resistance (Rct) between the carbonized silk fabrics and the electrolyte, as represented by the diameter of the semicircle in the EIS spectra. A smaller Rct implies faster charge transfer kinetics, which plays a pronounced role in the HER process. According to Fig. 10d, CSM-1050–0.5 had the smallest Rct among all samples, which contributed to its enhanced HER catalytic activity [61]. At equivalent sodium molybdate loading dosages, the carbonized silk fabrics fabricated under 1050 °C demonstrated a reduced charge transfer resistance (Rct) value compared to that fabricated under 850 °C. This phenomenon may be attributed to the increased graphitization degree observed in the CSM-1050-C, thereby enhancing conductivity. Furthermore, under the same carbonization temperature, the Rct value of the carbonized silk fabrics decreased with increasing sodium molybdate loading dosages. This trend is likely attributed to the amplified molybdenum carbide, with excellent electrical conductivity [62], generated as a result of the increased sodium molybdate loading dosages [63]. Moreover, the specific surface area, determined by the surface morphology of sample, can also contribute to the Rct values of the samples. An enlarged specific surface area facilitates contact between electrode and electrolyte, thereby decreasing Rct [64]. The EIS fitting data for various samples can be found in Table S6. Figure S6 shows the EIS spectra of sample CSM-1050–0.5 at different potentials, where increasing potential leads to a reduction in Rct, indicating that the sample is favorable for HER [65].

Long-term stability is a crucial criterion for how well CSM-1050–0.5 maintains its activity and efficiency over an extended period of operation. Here, durability studies were conducted through a 50-hour chronopotentiometry test at a constant current density of 100 mA·cm−2. According to the results, the overpotentials for CSM-1050–0.5 and commercial 40 wt% Pt/C increased by 77.25 mV and 249.86 mV, respectively (Fig. 10 e-f). This demonstrates that CSM-1050–0.5 possesses better stability than commercial 40 wt% Pt/C and is suitable for practical applications. SEM observations revealed that the nanoparticles attached to the carbonized silk fibers were retained despite continuous formation and fast release of bubbles on the surface during HER (Supplementary Video 1), indicating the structural stability of sample CSM-1050–0.5. According to contact angle measurements (Supplementary Materials: Figure S7), the hydrophilic surface of CSM-1050–0.5 facilitated the release of hydrogen bubbles from the surface [66–68].

In addition, the stability of CSM-1050–0.5 and commercial 40 wt% Pt/C was assessed through 1500 CV cycles in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution; the samples exhibited negative shifts of 9 mV and 10 mV in η10, respectively (Fig. 10 g-h). Therefore, CSM-1050–0.5 demonstrated good stability, comparable to that of commercial 40 wt% Pt/C. This performance can be attributed to the stable and catalytically active Mo2C as well as the anti-corrosion property of the carbon substrate derived from silk [69].

The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), indicative of the quantity of active sites on an electrode surface, can be calculated using the following equation [10]:
ECSA =
C
dl
C S
where Cdl represents the double-layer capacitance determined from CVs (Supplementary Materials: Figure S8), and Cs is the capacitance per unit area of the atomically smooth planar surface of the material. In the literature, Cs is 0.035 mF·cm−2 in an acidic solution. Table 2 shows that CSM-1050–0.5 has the largest ECSA among the carbonized silk fabrics.

Fig. 11 a illustrates the LSV curves of the commercial 40 wt% Pt/C catalyst and CSM-T-C in a 1 M KOH solution. Similar to the findings in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, CSM-1050–0.5 demonstrates remarkable catalytic activity for the HER among all prepared samples. In addition, the η10 of CSM-T-C and the commercial 40 wt% Pt/C electrocatalyst is higher under alkaline conditions than under acidic conditions. This observation can be attributed to the presence of hydroxide ions (OH–) in alkaline solutions, leading to increased ionization of water molecules and posing greater challenges to electron transfer, thereby heightening the overpotential [70].

Fig. 11c depicts the Tafel slopes of the prepared samples and commercial 40 wt% Pt/C in alkaline conditions. Among all prepared samples, CSM-1050–0.5 exhibited the smallest Tafel slope, indicating the fastest reaction kinetics. The order of the Tafel slope for all samples in the alkaline environment aligns with that observed in the acidic environment. In addition, the analysis revealed that the carbonized silk fabric exhibited a Tafel slope close to that of 40 wt% Pt/C in alkaline conditions, suggesting a favorable kinetic pathway for the HER. As illustrated in Fig. 11d, the diameter of the semicircle in the EIS spectrum of CSM-1050–0.5 was smaller than that of other sample groups, implying that CSM-1050–0.5 possesses the lowest charge transfer resistance and the fastest charge transfer rate. The EIS spectra were fitted using the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 11d, and the fitting data were listed in Table S7. The EIS spectra obtained in alkaline conditions at different potentials also confirm the excellent electrocatalytic properties of the material for the HER (Figure S9).

Fig. 11e-h reveal the sustained stability of CSM-1050–0.5 in facilitating the HER under alkaline conditions, as well as its superior durability compared to commercial 40 wt% Pt/C. From the data presented in Figure S10, the values of Cdl and ECSA for each sample in an alkaline solution can be quantified sequentially (Table 3 ). Notably, CSM-1050–0.5 demonstrates the highest Cdl and ECSA values, indicative of its exceptional electrocatalytic performance (Supplementary Video 2 presents the H2 bubble release).

In comparison with the electrocatalytic performance of other flexible electrodes and electrodes prepared based on Bombyx mori silk, for the HER, the electrode constructed in this work demonstrates comparable performance (Tables S8 and S9). It should be noted that many flexible HER electrodes currently reported rely on precursors from chemicals, and the preparation process often involves the use of organic solvents and multiple steps. By contrast, we used Bombyx mori silk, a sustainable resource, as the precursor for electrode preparation, ensuring the fabrication process was simple and free of organic solvents and providing a green synthesis pathway for flexible HER electrode fabrication. Moreover, silk fabric is cost-effective (approximately $12 USD per square meter) compared to other substrates commonly used for flexible electrode construction, such as carbon cloth (approximately $875 USD per square meter) and stainless steel (approximately $40 USD per square meter)[71].

Evaluating the potential toxicity of the carbonized silk fabrics is crucial because the HER electrode requires extended immersion in solution. As the yield discharge of the HER might come into contact with human beings, HEK-293 cells were employed to determine whether the prepared electrode is biologically safe. As shown in Fig. 12 , the cells of the control group and groups CSM-1050–0 and CSM-1050–0.5 exhibited great adhesion and similar morphology with clear edges. In addition, the increase over time in the number of cells in these groups is clearly apparent. By contrast, it is evident that the cells in the positive group gradually lost their morphology and became spherical. The number of cells decreased over time due to detachment and lysis processes. As shown in Fig. 13 , no statistically significant difference in cell viability exists between the experimental and control groups (both P > 0.05), suggesting that samples CSM-1050–0 and CSM-1050–0.5 exhibited no apparent cytotoxicity.

Electrocatalytic water-splitting stands as an eco-friendly, sustainable, and efficient methodology for hydrogen generation. Nonetheless, it encounters challenges, notably the exorbitant cost of conventional precious metal catalysts. In this investigation, we successfully engineered a silk-based flexible HER electrode featuring in situ grown nanoparticles, specifically composed of Mo2C and MoO3, considered as non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts, through the carbonization of molybdate-loaded silk fabric. The cost of this method is significantly lower than that of preparing precious metal catalysts. The ease with which sodium molybdate disperses in water and its ability to be readily loaded onto the silk fabric make this a viable option for large-scale applications.

The resultant carbonized silk fabric exhibits remarkable flexibility, enhancing shape adaptability and promoting a higher density of catalytic active sites for the HER within a confined space. Experimental findings reveal that the sodium molybdate dosage and carbonization temperature are directly proportional to the Mo2C content and HER performance of the carbonized silk fabric. In addition, a greater amount of loaded sodium molybdate and higher carbonization temperature can augment the specific surface area and heighten the graphitization degree of the carbonized silk fabrics, thereby synergistically enhancing the HER catalytic efficacy.

TEM analysis reveals the presence of carbon quantum dots in CSM-1050–0.5, contributing to the heightened electrocatalytic HER capability. The η10 values of CSM-1050–0.5 under acidic and alkaline conditions are determined to be 169.05 ± 15.26 mV and 212.88 ± 6.43 mV, respectively. Benefitting from the stability of Mo2C and corrosion-resistant carbon-based materials, CSM-1050–0.5 demonstrates exceptional stability and durability in acidic and alkaline environments. Cell viability assays confirm the non-cytotoxic nature of CSM-1050–0.5, rendering it suitable for extensive practical applications.

In summary, this investigation introduces a pioneering strategy for fabricating flexible HER electrodes with outstanding catalytic hydrogen evolution performance, demonstrating remarkable stability and applicability in acidic and alkaline conditions. The prepared electrodes may serve as a large-scale hydrogen production alternative to commercial Pt/C catalysts, thereby reducing hydrogen usage costs and supporting the goal of achieving net zero emissions. Future research could further refine the concentrations range of the sodium molybdate solutions, and elucidate the precise correlation between the dosage of sodium molybdate in solution and the molybdenum carbide loading. These efforts aim to enhance the applicability of the strategy in practical settings.

Jialong Wu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaoning Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Fucheng Ju: Data curation, Investigation, Software. Can Huang: Software, Formal analysis. Carrie Donley: Software, Methodology. Peng Xiao: Methodology. Lan Xu: Supervision. Hongju Zhang: Supervision.
